title: Structures of Identical Particle Systems : Efficient Computation of Many-Body Density of States
authors: Hovan Lee, Rémi Lefèvre, Grégoire Ithier
abstract: We present a method for approximating the many-body density of states of a system of quantum identical particles, with a reduction of the computational cost by a combinatorial factor compared to the full calculation. This is carried out by considering an isolated quantum system of identical particles, and studying its non-interacting many-body spectrum through the use of a new approach based on a separation of universal combinatorial properties from the system-specific quantities. In this paper we focus on a practical computation method that leverages our formalism of many-body combinatorics, in order to perform an efficient numerical computation of the many-body density of states. In addition, this method provides further computational improvements by allowing most of the results to be cached in persistent storage and computed incrementally, making way for efficient use of parallelization and dynamic programming techniques. We give an extensive description of the method and provide several detailed examples of approximations of bosonic many-body density of states with tunable accuracy requirements. Lastly, we demonstrate how one such approximation can be used to recover Bose-Einstein-like distributions without any particle statistics assumptions.
summary: The paper introduces an efficient numerical framework for computing the many-body density of states for identical particle systems. By leveraging the algebraic symmetries of the system's universal combinatorial properties, the authors can approximate the spectrum with tunable accuracy and significantly reduced computational complexity. This allows for the recovery of statistical distributions, such as Bose-Einstein statistics, without prior assumptions about particle statistics.
main_problem: The combinatorial explosion of the many-body density of states (MBDoS) for identical particle systems, which makes exact calculations intractable as the number of particles and single-particle states increases.
main_result: A method that reduces computational cost by a combinatorial factor by separating universal combinatorial properties from system-specific quantities and using a hierarchical q-sector truncation scheme.
method: The approach uses SVD to separate universal properties, Galois theory to study symmetries in cyclotomic fields, and generating functions with transfer matrices for efficient counting. It also employs simulated annealing to optimize single-particle energy permutations to minimize information loss during truncation.
model_or_system: Isolated quantum systems of identical particles (bosons and fermions) in a finite-dimensional space with L single-particle levels and N particles.
key_observables: Many-body density of states (MBDoS), single-particle occupation numbers, and microcanonical inverse temperature.
important_parameters: Number of particles (N), number of single-particle states (L), occupancy limit (R), and single-particle energy spectrum (epsilon).
main_assumptions: The method assumes the postulate of equiprobability (microcanonical ensemble) and the ergodicity hypothesis; it also relies on a kernel width (Gamma) for smoothing the spectrum.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the distribution of universal spectrum components in the complex plane; Figure 2 illustrates the hierarchical sector flow and its similarity to block-spin renormalization; Figure 3 demonstrates the generating function expansion for specific degeneracy classes; Figure 4 and 5 compare the accuracy of different energy permutations and truncation levels using Kernel Density Estimation; Figure 6 and 7 show the MBDoS, temperature estimates, and recovered particle distributions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the computational problem, presents a mathematical framework based on SVD and Galois theory to identify universal spectra, develops a generating function method for counting, proposes a hierarchical truncation scheme for efficiency, optimizes this scheme via simulated annealing, and validates the method by recovering Bose-Einstein distributions.
why_interesting: It provides a highly efficient way to compute many-body spectra and thermodynamic properties (like temperature and occupation numbers) for large systems without the need for expensive Monte Carlo sampling, which is directly applicable to analyzing the microcanonical ensemble in cold atom or trapped ion experiments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Degeneracies in the distribution of the U L ℓ components in the complex plane for L = 12, N = 4 and ℓ= 1 in the bosonic case. The color of each point repre- sents the degeneracy at this particular UL ℓ(n) value with exact counts given in the legend. In previous work [1], we introduced k- Symmetry and ℓ-Symmetry in these UL ℓdistributions which char- acterize universal properties of the combinatorics of many-body quantum systems of identical particles.

Figure 2. Analogy between the flow of q-sectors and Block-Spin renormalization on a lattice. We show the L = 20 sector flow diagram in the center (d) and detail how the folding from the q = 20 sector to both the q = 10 and q = 4 sectors occurs under the action of σq 2 and σq 5 respectively. Diagrams for the UL ℓ distributions with N = 2 corresponding to those sectors are shown with color-coding to indicate how degeneracy classes from U20 1 (a) are merged under the action of σq 2 to yield U20 2 (c) and the action of σq 5 to yield U20 5 (b). Figure 3. Illustration of the L = 6, N = 3, l = 2 U L l distribution, the degeneracy classes are color coded to denote their multiplicity, and labeled with the invariant values. These degeneracy values and invariant values can be compared to the terms in eq. (14), where the I3 0, I3 1 values are encoded as the power of Y0 and Y1 respectively, and the degeneracy is the integer value of the coefficients associated with the terms.

Figure 4. Many-body spectrum of a system with L = 20 number of single-body energy levels, and N = 6 number of bosonic particles. Left: a plot of the single-body energies. Middle: hierarchical approximations of the many-body spectrum (with monotonic single-body energy ordering ϵ), starting at the top with all q-sectors of the system, then successively discarding the largest q-valued sectors. With all q-sectors — including all modes of the universal spectrum — considered, the MBDoS is non-degenerate, as shown in blue. As q-sectors are discarded, the approximation of the MBDoS becomes more coarse, and the approximated many-body energy levels become more degenerate. Right: because the...

Figure 5. Kernel density estimations (KDEs) calculated from the full MBDoS and its approximations accounting for the q = 1, 2, 4, 5, 10-sectors with single-body for energy vectors ϵ, ϵ′ as shown in fig. 4. The KDE of the full un-approximated MBDoS is shown in dashed blue, and can be compared to those of the approximations yielded by ϵ, and ϵ′. The standard deviation of the Gaussian kernel was chosen to be 1000∆, which is 1000 times the mean level spacing across the entirety of the MBDoS.
